items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Two stocks, 40% of S&P 500 earnings growth this quarter
items.idea: Goldman's Q2 earnings preview: AI infrastructure names are expected to contribute ~60% of aggregate S&P 500 EPS growth, with the top 10 contributors accounting for ~75% of it. NVIDIA and Micron alone represent more than 40%. The S&P 500 is up ~18% over 12 months while the forward P/E has stayed near 20x — meaning the entire rally has been earnings-driven, not multiple expansion.
items.angle: If NVDA/MU miss by even a little, the whole index EPS growth narrative craters — this is effectively a two-stock market wearing an S&P 500 costume.
items.tweetA: NVDA and MU alone account for more than 40% of expected S&P 500 EPS growth this quarter. The other 498 companies are basically along for the ride. Forward P/E hasn't expanded in 12 months — the entire rally is earnings, and the earnings are two stocks. $NVDA $MU $SPY
items.tweetB: Everyone says this rally isn't a bubble because P/E hasn't re-rated. True — but nobody mentions the earnings backing it up are ~75% concentrated in 10 names. That's not breadth, that's leverage on a very short list. $SPX

charts.desc: One embedded chart on AI infra earnings contribution to S&P 500 EPS growth.

items.title: CLARITY Act deadline hits today — BTC ETFs bleeding for 6 straight weeks anyway
items.idea: Spot BTC ETFs just logged a 6th consecutive week of outflows totaling $5.94B cumulative. BTC fell from a weekly high near $62,900 to ~$57,700; Fear & Greed sits at 24 (Extreme Fear). Today, July 4, is Trump's self-imposed deadline for the CLARITY Act — Galaxy Research puts 2026 passage odds at roughly 50-50, and it still needs 60 Senate votes plus reconciliation.
items.angle: Regulatory clarity was supposed to be the crypto unlock this year. Six weeks of ETF outflows into the exact week of the deadline says the market isn't waiting for Congress to tell it what to do.
items.tweetA: Spot BTC ETFs just posted their 6th straight week of outflows — $5.94B cumulative — while Fear & Greed sits at 24. Six months ago this setup would've been called capitulation and a buy signal. Nobody's saying that this week. $BTC
items.tweetB: The CLARITY Act deadline is today, July 4, and BTC is still stuck near $57.7k with ETFs bleeding for six weeks straight. Regulatory clarity was supposed to be the unlock — so far the market's shrugging at it entirely. $BTC

charts.desc: BTC/ETH/miner price and options-flow charts referenced; ETF flow chart likely inside.

items.title: Strategy (MSTR) just ended its 'never sell' era
items.idea: Strategy announced a new 'Digital Credit Capital Framework' on June 29, authorizing up to $1.25B in Bitcoin sales to build cash reserves, plus a $1B buyback and a hike in the STRC dividend to 12%. The company bought zero Bitcoin last week — its first pause in years. MSTR shares rose ~13% on the news.
items.angle: The company built entirely on Saylor's 'never sell' doctrine just authorized selling, and the market rewarded it. That's a structural inversion of the whole MicroStrategy trade.
items.tweetA: Strategy just authorized selling up to $1.25B of its Bitcoin — the first crack in Saylor's 'never sell' doctrine since the strategy began. MSTR stock rallied 13% on the news. The market just told you it prefers a solvent balance sheet to a religious one. $MSTR $BTC
items.tweetB: MSTR bought zero Bitcoin last week for the first time in years, right as it authorizes a $1B buyback partly funded by BTC sales. If the largest corporate BTC holder becomes a net seller, who's the marginal buyer left in this market? $MSTR $BTC

charts.desc: No dedicated chart in-email; MSTR price action referenced in text.

items.title: ETH staking hits a record 31% — but it's not retail buying it
items.idea: Ethereum's staking ratio hit 31% (~37.3M ETH staked), up from ~29% in early 2025. But recent validator growth is concentrated among large entities and ETFs rather than retail, and net staking flows have turned negative at times. ETH has lagged Bitcoin through the entire selloff.
items.angle: Rising staking ratio usually signals reduced liquid supply and is read as bullish — but if it's institutional custody parking rather than organic retail conviction, it's a fragile floor, not a demand signal.
items.tweetA: ETH staking ratio hit a record 31%, which sounds bullish until you see who's staking: large entities and ETFs, not retail. Concentrated staking isn't reduced float, it's custodial parking — and it can reverse a lot faster than the headline ratio suggests. $ETH
items.tweetB: ETH has lagged BTC through this entire selloff despite a record staking ratio. If 'less liquid supply' isn't showing up in relative price strength, the staking number is telling you less about demand than everyone assumes. $ETH $BTC

charts.desc: No chart embedded for this specific data point.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: A flagship Virginia AI data center just died in zoning court
items.idea: The Prince William Digital Gateway in Northern Virginia appears to have collapsed after court challenges, zoning disputes, and public opposition — a procedural failure in the public-notice process invalidated its approvals after years of local resistance. The author's read: existing powered and leased data centers become more valuable as greenfield development gets harder, and the constraint is shifting from capex to grid, permitting, and local politics.
items.angle: The physical bottleneck nobody prices into AI capex models: you can raise unlimited capital for GPUs but you can't zone your way past a hostile county board.
items.tweetA: A flagship Virginia data center project just collapsed on a zoning technicality, not a funding problem. The AI capex story keeps getting modeled as a compute question — it's actually a land-use and grid-interconnection question, and those move on county-government time, not GPU-cycle time.
items.tweetB: If greenfield AI data center sites keep dying in zoning court, the real scarcity asset isn't the GPU — it's the already-powered, already-permitted shell. Existing leased data center capacity should be repricing higher relative to greenfield land banks. $DLR $EQIX

charts.desc: One supporting image; article is text/case-study driven.

items.title: BTC got bipartisan Senate progress and got rejected at its 200-day MA anyway
items.idea: Despite the Senate Banking Committee advancing the Digital Asset Market Clarity Act 15-9 in a bipartisan vote weeks ago, BTC has been rejected at its 200-day moving average twice and is now also below its 200-week MA — trading near $62,819, down 29.2% YTD, with $74k flagged as the key overhead level aligning with the 200 SMA.
items.angle: Good regulatory news plus bad price action is the market telling you regulation was never the binding constraint on this cycle — something else (liquidity, rates) is.
items.tweetA: BTC got a bipartisan 15-9 Senate committee vote advancing crypto market-structure legislation and responded by getting rejected at its 200-day moving average twice. If regulatory clarity can't move price, stop pricing the next rally on the assumption that it will. $BTC
items.tweetB: Bitcoin is down 29% YTD and just lost its 200-week moving average for the first time this cycle. Everyone's watching the Clarity Act. The chart is watching liquidity, and liquidity isn't listening to Congress right now. $BTC

charts.desc: Full technical chart pack: SPX, COMPQ, BTC, EEM, GOLD, MU, SOXX, LLY, XBI plus breadth and earnings infographics.

items.title: Gold lost its 200-day moving average for the first time since the 2023 bull run began
items.idea: Gold trades at $4,122.76, down 6.89% below its 50-day SMA and 7.59% below its 200-day SMA — the first loss of the 200-day MA since the bull run started in late 2023. The author flags $4,100 as the level bulls need to hold, and says he'd add physical gold on a move to the $3,700-3,900 zone.
items.angle: A structural bull market losing its 200-day MA for the first time in 2.5 years is a real technical tell — and a PM saying he'd buy 10% lower is itself a positioning signal worth noting.
items.tweetA: Gold just lost its 200-day moving average for the first time since the 2023 bull run started. Down 4.6% YTD, that's easy to miss under the AI and crypto headlines — the metal quietly went from the best trade of 2024-25 to a broken chart in a few months. $GLD
items.tweetB: One trader on record says he adds physical gold at $3,700-3,900 — that's another 10-12% below spot. When the bulls' own buy zone requires a double-digit further drawdown, that's not a dip, that's a re-rating in progress. $GLD

charts.desc: Gold chart with 50/200 SMA overlay inside the full technical pack — clean visual of the MA breakdown.

items.title: The exact stocks carrying S&P earnings growth are the first ones cracking technically
items.idea: Micron (MU) is up 209% YTD and 119.6% above its 200-day SMA, but has shown high-volume distribution days with a bearish RSI divergence since early June, losing its 21-day EMA for the first time since mid-March. SOXX broke below its 21-day EMA on above-average volume after an 80.73% YTD run.
items.angle: Read against item #1 (NVDA+MU = 40%+ of S&P earnings growth), the exact names carrying the index's earnings story are the first to show technical cracks — worth watching whether this is healthy rotation or something bigger.
items.tweetA: Micron is up 209% YTD and just posted its first high-volume distribution days with a bearish RSI divergence since the run started. SOXX broke its 21-day EMA on above-average volume the same week. The exact stocks carrying 40%+ of S&P earnings growth are the first ones showing technical cracks. $MU $SOXX
items.tweetB: Semis (SOXX, +80% YTD) just broke below the 21-day EMA on heavy volume while the S&P and Nasdaq made new highs on breadth. That reads like a rotation, not a crash — money leaving the crowded winner, not leaving equities. $SOXX $SPY

charts.desc: MU and SOXX charts with EMA/volume annotations in the full pack.

items.title: S&P breadth just hit 67% in the same week semis broke down
items.idea: The percentage of S&P 500 stocks above their 50-day moving average rose to 67% this week. Dow +2.31%, S&P +2.46%, Nasdaq +3.13% on the week, even as the semis/AI complex showed technical weakness (see MU/SOXX above).
items.angle: Paired with the semis breakdown, this looks like the actual 'broadening' trade strategists have been calling for since 2023 — capital rotating into the other ~490 stocks while mega-cap AI cools.
items.tweetA: S&P 500 breadth (% of stocks above the 50-day MA) climbed to 67% this week, the same week semis broke down technically. That's the broadening trade — money isn't leaving equities, it's leaving the 10 stocks everyone already owns. $SPX $RSP
items.tweetB: Everyone's called for 'market broadening' since 2023 and mostly been wrong. This week might be the actual signal: breadth up to 67%, AI/semis down on volume, indices still green. Watch if it holds past one week before believing it.

charts.desc: Breadth chart (% stocks above 50-day MA) included in the full technical pack.

items.title: McKinsey puts a hard number on quantum computing adoption: 300+ companies
items.idea: McKinsey's Quantum Technology Monitor 2026 reports quantum computing has passed a 'commercial tipping point,' with over 300 global companies now adopting it, fueling what the report calls a multibillion-dollar market.
items.angle: Quantum has been '5 years away' for a decade; a named adopter count is a concrete number to track quarter over quarter instead of vague TAM slides.
items.tweetA: McKinsey just put a number on quantum computing adoption: 300+ companies now using it commercially, not experimentally. Every prior 'quantum is close' claim was vibes. This one has a headcount attached — worth tracking quarter over quarter.
items.tweetB: Quantum computing's 'multibillion-dollar market' claim means less than the adopter count next to it: 300 companies. Compare that to where cloud adoption sat around 2010 — the number that matters isn't the TAM slide, it's how fast 300 becomes 1,000. $IBM $IONQ

charts.desc: Roundup email with no charts; links out to the full Quantum Technology Monitor report.

items.title: 'AI is everywhere. The agentic organization isn't — yet.'
items.idea: McKinsey's 'AI transformation manifesto' argues most companies are experimenting with AI but few have realized value from it — the gap isn't the technology, it's redesigning workflows, leadership, and culture for an agentic organization. The report lists 12 themes separating 'rewired' companies from peers.
items.angle: This is the practical bear case for near-term AI productivity showing up in earnings: the bottleneck has moved from model capability to organizational redesign, which is slow and unglamorous compared to a product launch.
items.tweetA: McKinsey's own framing: 'AI is everywhere, the agentic organization isn't — yet.' That's the consulting-speak version of saying enterprise AI ROI is stuck not because the models are bad, but because nobody's redesigned the org chart around them yet. Bottleneck moved from compute to management.
items.tweetB: If the AI productivity bottleneck is organizational redesign, not model quality, the earnings inflection everyone's waiting for won't come from Anthropic or OpenAI's next release — it'll come from whichever industry rewrites its workflows first. Watch the org charts, not the benchmarks.

charts.desc: Text-framework roundup; no charts in the email itself.

items.title: $21B exited gold funds in Q2 — window dressing or real capitulation?
items.idea: Vince Lanci of GoldFix, in conversation with Goldfinger Capital, discusses $21 billion exiting gold funds in Q2 2026, framed as end-of-quarter 'window dressing' liquidation that may have cleared the ground for a short-covering rally in early July.
items.angle: Set against SixSigma's read that gold's 200-day MA loss is a real technical breakdown (item above), this is a second source on the same asset with the opposite conclusion — mechanical outflow, not fundamental capitulation.
items.tweetA: $21B left gold funds in Q2. Read one way, that's fundamental capitulation. Read another, it's quarter-end window dressing that just cleared the order book for a short-covering bounce. The chart lost its 200-day MA either way — the flow data is what disagrees on why. $GLD
items.tweetB: Two gold analysts, same asset, opposite conclusions this week: one says the 200-day MA breakdown is the real signal, another says $21B of Q2 outflows was mechanical and sets up a bounce. When technicians and flow-watchers disagree this openly, that's usually close to where the bottom forms. $GLD

charts.desc: Video/podcast format — no static charts in the email itself.

items.title: Monday.com is buying back 26% of its own market cap
items.idea: MNDY: $3.3B market cap, $323M sustainable FCF (15%+ net-cash-adjusted FCF yield), $1.5B cash with a net cash position over $1B, and an $870M buyback authorization — roughly 26% of the entire market cap, with $554M already executed in Q1 2026 alone (4.5x the dilution from $177M in annual stock comp). ROIC is above 25%.
items.angle: A company buying back over a quarter of its own market cap while holding $1B+ net cash is either deeply undervalued or the market is pricing in a SaaS-obsolescence scenario that management itself clearly doesn't believe.
items.tweetA: Monday.com is buying back 26% of its own market cap while holding $1B+ net cash and printing a 15% FCF yield. Either the 'AI kills SaaS' trade is wrong, or management is about to look very foolish — but insiders don't usually retire a quarter of the float on their way to zero. $MNDY
items.tweetB: $554M of an $870M buyback already executed in one quarter, against $177M in annual stock comp — that's 4.5x the offset ratio most 'shareholder-friendly' SaaS companies claim and never deliver. Compare MNDY's actual buyback pace to any mega-cap's before believing the next earnings call. $MNDY

charts.desc: Financial breakdown charts/tables on FCF, buyback pace, and balance sheet.

items.title: The 'CAIR' framework flips the SaaS-pocalypse thesis on its head
items.idea: A former monday.com exec (now at Nebius) and LangChain's Harrison Chase built the 'CAIR' (Confidence in AI Results) framework, arguing the market's fear that AI agents kill seat-based SaaS ignores how enterprises actually adopt technology — an untethered agent that hallucinates, deletes data, or emails a client the wrong quote is where the risk 'denominator explodes' without a governance layer.
items.angle: The bear case on SaaS assumes agents replace software outright; CAIR flips it — ungoverned agents need exactly the kind of system-of-record/custody layer that incumbent SaaS platforms already are, which is an argument for incumbents as AI beneficiaries, not casualties.
items.tweetA: The 'AI kills SaaS' thesis assumes agents replace the software. The counter-framework from an ex-monday.com exec and LangChain's Harrison Chase: an ungoverned agent that hallucinates inside your business is a liability generator, not a cost saver. The platform with the custody layer is what makes agents safe to deploy — that's a moat, not a threat. $MNDY
items.tweetB: Nobody prices the tail risk of the 'AI replaces seat-based software' trade: what's the cost when an untethered agent emails a client the wrong quote or deletes a record? Until that's answered, procurement buys the governed platform, not the raw agent. Software with a system of record just got more valuable, not less.

charts.desc: CAIR framework diagram illustrating error-denominator risk for ungoverned agents.

items.title: A gold-bull macro letter picks July 4th to make the debt-monetization case
items.idea: Tavi Costa's open letter argues that as US government debt has expanded dramatically over decades, America's traditional monetary anchors have shrunk relative to the size of its obligations — framed around institutional trust and monetary/fiscal discipline as the actual long-run competitive advantage of nations, more than innovation or military strength.
items.angle: This is standard hard-money framing repackaged for a mainstream, patriotic-holiday audience — worth noting as a sentiment/marketing signal from the gold-bull community as much as a data claim.
items.tweetA: A gold-bull macro letter picked July 4th to make the debt-monetization case, dressed as a tribute to American institutions. When hard-money arguments start wrapping themselves in patriotism, that's usually the marketing cycle catching up to the positioning, not new data driving it.
items.tweetB: The actual claim buried in the patriotism: US monetary anchors have shrunk relative to $39T+ in obligations for decades. True and well-known. The question worth asking is why every gold manager is re-making this exact argument in the same week gold just lost its 200-day MA. $GLD

charts.desc: One chart referenced on monetary anchors vs. government debt growth.

items.title: Claude Sonnet 5 ships near-Opus performance at a lower price
items.idea: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5, described as its most agentic Sonnet yet — planning, driving browsers and terminals, and checking its own output. Anthropic says performance is close to Opus 4.8 on reasoning, tool use, coding, and knowledge work at a lower price point, now default across Free/Pro and available on Max/Team/Enterprise, with introductory pricing through August.
items.angle: The pattern of near-flagship performance compressing into a mid-tier price is the actual deflationary force in the AI trade — it eventually shows up as margin pressure for anyone monetizing per-token, not anyone monetizing compute.
items.tweetA: Anthropic just shipped a mid-tier model (Sonnet 5) performing near their flagship (Opus 4.8) at a lower price. That's the compression cycle every model vendor eventually hits — yesterday's frontier becomes this quarter's default tier. Bearish for anyone monetizing a per-token markup, bullish for anyone monetizing outcomes. $GOOG $MSFT
items.tweetB: The interesting number isn't the benchmark, it's 'intro pricing through August' — that's a company racing to lock in agentic workflow volume before a probable price-war response from OpenAI or Google. Token pricing is heading the way of cloud storage: down, fast, permanently.

charts.desc: Benchmark comparison graphics for Sonnet 5 vs. prior models.

items.title: xAI's voice agent pricing just closed a 6-10x unit-cost gap on call centers
items.idea: xAI launched a no-code Voice Agent Builder running on Grok Voice, priced at $0.05 per minute, aimed at customer support, sales, and other high-volume voice workflows.
items.angle: $0.05/min is directly comparable to a loaded human call-center minute (~$0.30-0.50 depending on geography) — that's a concrete unit-economics gap wide enough to move headcount decisions, not just a productivity narrative.
items.tweetA: xAI priced its voice agent at $0.05/minute. A loaded human call-center minute runs $0.30-0.50 depending on geography. That's not 'AI helps agents be more productive' economics — that's a 6-10x unit-cost gap wide enough to actually move headcount, not just augment it. BPO names should be watching this number, not the model benchmarks.
items.tweetB: Nobody's talking about the pricing on xAI's new voice agent product — $0.05/minute, no-code. That's the number that turns 'AI voice agents are coming' into 'AI voice agents are cheaper than the alternative today.' The gap between narrative and unit economics just closed for an entire industry.

charts.desc: No chart; product-launch bullet format.

items.title: June payrolls: 57k vs 115k expected, and the 'improvement' is an illusion
items.idea: June payrolls came in at 57,000 versus 115,000 expected, down from May's revised 129,000. Unemployment 'improved' to 4.2% only because labor force participation fell to 61.5% (lowest since March 2021) — the household survey lost 507,000 jobs in a single month. Leisure and hospitality lost 61,000 jobs despite a widely modeled World Cup hiring bump that never showed up. Wages stayed sticky: +0.3% m/m, +3.5% y/y.
items.angle: A falling unemployment rate built entirely on shrinking participation, next to still-sticky wage growth, is the stagflation-adjacent combination that boxes the Fed in on both sides.
items.tweetA: June payrolls: 57k vs 115k expected. The unemployment rate 'improved' to 4.2% only because 507,000 people left the household survey and participation fell to a 5-year low. That's not a strong labor market printing a good number, that's a shrinking one printing a flattering ratio. Wages still up 3.5% y/y — the Fed can't cut on this without owning the inflation number too.
items.tweetB: Leisure and hospitality lost 61,000 jobs in June, the exact month everyone modeled a World Cup hiring bump. When the seasonal tailwind fails to show up in the one sector built to catch it, that's a demand problem, not a data quirk.

charts.desc: Labor market breakdown chart among the 10-chart pack in this letter.

items.title: The Fed just turned hawkish at the exact moment inflation swaps rolled over
items.idea: The Fed's tone, measured speech by speech, has swung decisively hawkish at the precise moment 1-year inflation swaps rolled over — tightening rhetoric into falling market-implied inflation expectations rather than rising ones. The letter draws a parallel to Q4 2018 (2yr yield 1.9%→2.9%, S&P -19.8% into Christmas Eve) and 2022 (2yr 0.7%→4.4%, S&P -19.4%, Nasdaq -33%).
items.angle: Central banks are supposed to tighten into rising inflation; this one is tightening its language into falling inflation swaps — the same shape that preceded two double-digit drawdowns in the last decade.
items.tweetA: The Fed's rhetoric just turned hawkish at the exact moment 1-year inflation swaps rolled over — tightening into falling inflation expectations, not rising ones. That exact sequence preceded a 19.8% S&P drawdown in Q4 2018 and a 19.4% one in 2022. Doesn't mean it repeats, but it's the same shape twice before it did.
items.tweetB: Central banks are supposed to tighten into rising inflation. This Fed is tightening its language into falling inflation swaps. If that's not a policy error waiting to be admitted, someone should explain what game they think they're playing.

charts.desc: Chart overlaying Fed hawkishness score vs. 1-year inflation swaps — the crossing lines are the whole thesis in one image.

items.title: The dollar just swept every single G10 currency in one month
items.idea: The dollar outpaced every G10 currency this month: yen -1.47%, GBP -1.87%, EUR -2.38%, DKK -2.42%, CAD -2.80%, CHF -3.41%, AUD -4.01%, SEK -4.94%, NZD -5.68%, and the Norwegian krone -6.37%. The author's rule of thumb: every sustained 10% dollar rally has historically stripped an estimated 2-4% off S&P earnings, concentrated in multinationals.
items.angle: A clean sweep against every G10 currency is rare and mechanically bearish for exactly the mega-cap multinational earnings currently propping up the index — the same names flagged in item #1's AI-capex EPS concentration.
items.tweetA: The dollar didn't just beat one rival currency this month — it beat all ten G10 currencies, with the Norwegian krone down 6.37% alone. A sweep this clean has a mechanical cost: every sustained 10% dollar rally has historically stripped 2-4% off S&P earnings, concentrated in the same multinationals carrying this rally. $DXY $UUP
items.tweetB: If NVDA and MU already account for 40%+ of S&P earnings growth this quarter, and the dollar just swept every G10 currency, ask what a stronger dollar does to multinational translation math before assuming the earnings beat is safe. Two separate newsletters, same week, same collision course. $DXY

charts.desc: G10 currency performance bar chart showing the full sweep.

items.title: The Fed owns just 14.17% of Treasuries — while debt crosses $39.3 trillion
items.idea: The Federal Reserve now owns just 14.17% of all outstanding US Treasuries, down from nearly 25% at the 2021 peak, while US government debt just crossed $39.3 trillion and keeps compounding with no visible inflection. The letter frames this as the 'buyer of last resort' shrinking exactly as issuance accelerates — a gap that historically resolves via the Fed returning (Sept 2019 repo spike, March 2020 QE).
items.angle: This is the structural mechanism behind every 'debt monetization is coming back' argument (including Tavi Costa's, above) — distinct from the sentiment wrapper, this is the actual math.
items.tweetA: The Fed now holds just 14.17% of outstanding Treasuries, down from 25% in 2021, while US debt just crossed $39.3T and keeps compounding. The last two times the 'buyer of last resort' shrank this much this fast — Sept 2019 repo spike, March 2020 — the Fed came back within months. The math doesn't care about the timeline, only the gap.
items.tweetB: $39.3T in debt, a shrinking Fed balance sheet, and private buyers left holding the marginal auction. That gap resolves one of three ways: higher yields, a weaker long bond, or the Fed un-retiring as buyer of last resort. Pick which one you're not positioned for. $TLT

charts.desc: Chart of Fed share of outstanding Treasuries (25%→14.17%) overlaid against total debt ($39.3T and rising) — the single clearest chart in the letter.
